medications: Isotretinoin (Accutane, Absorica, Amnesteem, Claravis, Myorisan, Zenatane)
requirements: Prescriber must be registered and activated in iPLEDGE
Female patients of reproductive potential: 2 negative pregnancy tests before starting, monthly pregnancy tests during, 1 month after
Two forms of contraception required for female patients of reproductive potential
Monthly authorization required before each prescription fill
Patient must sign informed consent/patient agreement
Pharmacy must verify authorization before dispensing
30-day prescription limit; 7-day pickup window

monitoring_labs.interval: Before treatment, then as clinically indicated
Before treatment, then as clinically indicated

notes: iPLEDGE applies to ALL isotretinoin products regardless of manufacturer. Program has separate requirements for male patients, females of reproductive potential, and females not of reproductive potential.

medications: Includes: OxyContin, MS Contin, Duragesic (fentanyl patch), Opana ER, Nucynta ER, Hysingla ER, Xtampza ER, methadone, Embeda, morphine sulfate ER, hydromorphone ER
requirements: Manufacturers must make training available to prescribers (CE/CME-based education)
Prescriber training covers: proper patient selection, dosing, monitoring, and counseling
Patient counseling required on risks, safe use, storage, and disposal
Provide Medication Guide to patient with each dispensing
Training is available but NOT mandatory for prescribers (voluntary)
notes: The Opioid Analgesic REMS is primarily an education-based program. It does NOT require prescriber certification or patient enrollment in a registry. However, best practice includes screening with PDMP, urine drug testing, and opioid risk tools (ORT, SOAPP-R). Individual state laws may impose additional requirements.

medications: Subsys (fentanyl sublingual spray) — removed from market but REMS still active for generics
requirements: Prescriber MUST be enrolled in TIRF REMS Access program
Only for opioid-TOLERANT patients with breakthrough cancer pain (or chronic pain if already on around-the-clock opioids)
Patient must sign Patient-Prescriber Agreement
Pharmacy must be enrolled in TIRF REMS
Outpatient use only — different requirements for inpatient
Prescriber must counsel patient on risks, proper use, storage, disposal
notes: TIRF products are NOT interchangeable. Each product has its own dosing titration. Prescribers must verify opioid tolerance before prescribing. Opioid tolerance defined as: taking ≥60 mg oral morphine/day, ≥25 mcg/hr transdermal fentanyl, ≥30 mg oral oxycodone/day, or equianalgesic dose of another opioid for ≥1 week.

status_detail: FDA removed the Clozapine REMS effective June 13, 2025. ANC monitoring is still recommended as standard of care but is no longer federally mandated through a REMS program.
requirements: REMS REMOVED June 13, 2025 — registry enrollment no longer required
ANC monitoring STILL RECOMMENDED as standard of care:
- First 6 months: ANC weekly
- Months 6-12: ANC every 2 weeks (if ANC ≥ 1500/µL in general population, ≥ 1000/µL in BEN population)
- After 12 months: ANC monthly (if continuously maintained)
Clozapine should be discontinued if ANC < 1000/µL (general) or < 500/µL (BEN)
Benign Ethnic Neutropenia (BEN): lower ANC thresholds apply; discuss with hematology
Although REMS removed, pharmacies may still require ANC before dispensing per institutional policy

monitoring_labs.lab: ANC (Absolute Neutrophil Count) via CBC with differential

monitoring_labs.interval: Baseline, then at 4 months, then annually
Baseline, then at 4 months, then annually

notes: Even though the REMS was removed, clozapine carries serious risks including agranulocytosis (cumulative incidence ~0.8%), myocarditis/cardiomyopathy, seizures, severe constipation/ileus, and metabolic syndrome. Continue monitoring as standard of care. Some pharmacies and health systems may maintain their own monitoring requirements. Document monitoring decisions in the chart.

requirements: Prescriber must be registered and certified in Thalomid REMS
Female patients of reproductive potential: pregnancy test 10-14 days prior and within 24 hours before starting
Weekly pregnancy testing during first 4 weeks, then every 4 weeks (regular cycles) or every 2 weeks (irregular cycles)
Two forms of contraception required, beginning 4 weeks before therapy
Male patients: must use latex condoms during any sexual contact — thalidomide is present in semen
Patient must sign informed consent acknowledging teratogenicity
28-day prescription limit; no automatic refills; no telephone prescriptions
Pharmacy must be registered; must verify authorization before dispensing
notes: Thalidomide is a known human teratogen. Even a single dose during pregnancy can cause severe birth defects or fetal death. The REMS is one of the most restrictive programs in existence. Prescribers must counsel patients that thalidomide must NEVER be shared and unused capsules must be returned to the pharmacy.

clinical_area: Oncology (Multiple Myeloma, MDS, Mantle Cell Lymphoma)
requirements: Prescriber must be registered and certified in Revlimid REMS
Structurally similar to thalidomide — same teratogenicity risk
Female patients of reproductive potential: 2 negative pregnancy tests before starting
Weekly pregnancy testing during first 4 weeks, then monthly (regular cycles) or every 2 weeks (irregular cycles)
Two forms of contraception required
Male patients: must use latex condoms — lenalidomide is present in semen
28-day prescription limit; patient survey required before each cycle
Pharmacy must be certified
notes: Lenalidomide also carries risk of deep vein thrombosis (DVT) and pulmonary embolism (PE). Thromboprophylaxis recommended. Second primary malignancies have been observed. Monitor for tumor lysis syndrome in patients with high tumor burden.

requirements: Same structure as Thalomid/Revlimid REMS — prescriber certification, pregnancy testing, contraception
Female patients of reproductive potential: 2 negative pregnancy tests before starting
Weekly pregnancy testing during first 4 weeks, then monthly
Two forms of contraception required
Male patients: latex condoms required
28-day prescription limit
notes: Pomalidomide is a thalidomide analogue. All teratogenicity warnings apply. Also carries thromboembolism risk — thromboprophylaxis recommended.

medications: Calcium, magnesium, potassium, and sodium oxybates (Xywav)
requirements: Prescriber must be enrolled in Xyrem/Xywav REMS
Only available through the certified central pharmacy (Jazz Pharmaceuticals central pharmacy)
Patient must be enrolled in the REMS program
No samples, starter packs, or alternative distribution
Maximum 1-month supply per shipment
Patient counseling on: CNS depression, respiratory depression, abuse potential (Schedule III controlled substance — contains GHB)
notes: Sodium oxybate is the sodium salt of GHB (gamma-hydroxybutyrate). It carries risks of CNS/respiratory depression especially with other CNS depressants or alcohol. The central pharmacy distribution model is designed to minimize diversion. Xywav has lower sodium content than Xyrem.

requirements: Prescriber must be enrolled in TOUCH program
Patient must be enrolled and complete enrollment/re-enrollment questionnaire
Infusion center must be enrolled and certified
Administered only by IV infusion in a certified infusion center
Patient must be observed for ≥1 hour after each infusion
Assessment for signs/symptoms of PML before each infusion
JC virus antibody testing before and periodically during treatment
Brain MRI at or within 3 months of starting treatment

monitoring_labs.lab: JC virus antibody test (anti-JCV antibody index)

monitoring_labs.interval: Baseline, then at least annually (more frequently if JCV antibody positive)

notes: Natalizumab carries risk of PML (progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy), an often fatal opportunistic brain infection. Risk increases with: JCV antibody positivity (especially index > 1.5), treatment duration > 2 years, and prior immunosuppressant use. If PML is suspected, immediately withhold natalizumab and initiate diagnostic workup.

requirements: Prescriber must be enrolled in Lemtrada REMS
Healthcare facility must be enrolled and equipped to manage infusion reactions and autoimmune complications
Patient must be enrolled in patient monitoring program
Monitoring required for 48 MONTHS after last infusion course
Monthly blood and urine monitoring throughout the 48-month monitoring period

monitoring_labs.interval: Monthly for 48 months after last dose
Monthly for 48 months after last dose
Monthly for 48 months after last dose
Every 3 months for 48 months after last dose

notes: Alemtuzumab causes prolonged lymphocyte depletion. Serious autoimmune conditions can develop months to years after treatment including: immune thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP), anti-glomerular basement membrane disease (Goodpasture syndrome), and thyroid disorders. The 48-month monitoring period is one of the longest post-treatment monitoring requirements of any REMS.

requirements: Prescriber must complete a Tikosyn education program and be registered
Treatment must be INITIATED (or re-initiated) in a hospital setting with continuous ECG monitoring for ≥3 days
Pharmacy must verify prescriber registration before dispensing
Hospital pharmacy must stock Tikosyn to supply the first prescription
Dose must be individualized based on CrCl and QTc response

monitoring_labs.interval: 2-3 hours after each dose during initiation, then periodically

notes: Dofetilide can cause dose-dependent QTc prolongation and torsades de pointes. The REMS requires in-hospital initiation because the highest risk period for QTc prolongation is the first 3 days. Contraindicated if CrCl < 20 mL/min. Must avoid QTc-prolonging drug interactions (verapamil, cimetidine, ketoconazole, trimethoprim, etc.).

clinical_area: Neurology (Infantile Spasms, Refractory Complex Partial Seizures)
requirements: Prescriber, pharmacy, and patient must all be enrolled in Vigabatrin REMS (SHARE program)
Baseline visual field testing before or within 4 weeks of starting
Vision assessment every 3 months during treatment
Patient/caregiver counseling on risk of permanent vision loss
Prescriber must assess risk vs. benefit periodically

monitoring_labs.interval: Baseline, then every 3 months during treatment

notes: Vigabatrin causes permanent bilateral concentric visual field constriction in 30% or more of patients. Vision loss is typically not reversible even after drug discontinuation. In infants with infantile spasms, visual field testing is not possible — ophthalmologic assessment (ERG) recommended. Adults should be weighed off vigabatrin if no meaningful seizure reduction after 3 months.

requirements: Prescriber must be certified in Addyi REMS
Patient must be counseled on: risk of hypotension and syncope with alcohol use
Patient must be assessed for potential interactions with CYP3A4 inhibitors
Pharmacy must be certified and must counsel patient at each dispensing
notes: Flibanserin interacts dangerously with alcohol (severe hypotension and syncope). Also interacts with strong/moderate CYP3A4 inhibitors. Indicated only for premenopausal women with acquired, generalized hypoactive sexual desire disorder. Contraindicated with alcohol use, hepatic impairment, and CYP3A4 inhibitors.

requirements: Prescriber must be enrolled in Lotronex Prescribing Program
Only for women with severe diarrhea-predominant IBS (IBS-D) who have failed conventional therapy
Patient must sign Patient-Physician Agreement
Starting dose must be 0.5 mg BID; may increase to 1 mg BID after 4 weeks if needed
Discontinue immediately if constipation or signs of ischemic colitis develop
notes: Alosetron carries risk of serious GI adverse effects including ischemic colitis and serious complications of constipation, which have resulted in hospitalization, blood transfusion, surgery, and death. Patients should be counseled on warning signs and when to seek immediate medical attention.

requirements: Primarily a communication-based REMS (Medication Guide distribution)
Female patients of reproductive potential: negative pregnancy test before starting
Effective contraception required during treatment and for 6 weeks after stopping
Patient must receive and acknowledge Medication Guide
Prescriber should counsel on: pregnancy risks, infection risk, lymphoma risk

monitoring_labs.interval: Weekly × first month, twice monthly × months 2-3, then monthly × first year

notes: Mycophenolate is a known teratogen (FDA Pregnancy Category D / Category X in some formulations). First-trimester exposure associated with increased risk of spontaneous abortion and congenital malformations (ear, facial, cardiac, renal). The REMS focuses on pregnancy prevention communication rather than registry enrollment.

requirements: Prescriber and pharmacy must be enrolled
Female patients of reproductive potential: negative pregnancy test before starting and monthly thereafter
Effective contraception required during treatment and for 1 month after
Monthly LFT monitoring: AST/ALT must be checked before and monthly during treatment
Dispense only through specialty/enrolled pharmacies

monitoring_labs.interval: Baseline, at 1 and 3 months, then quarterly

notes: Bosentan is an endothelin receptor antagonist. Hepatotoxicity risk (elevated aminotransferases) — dose reduction or discontinuation required if AST/ALT > 3× ULN. Also decreases hemoglobin (usually within first few weeks). Known teratogen. Drug interactions: strong CYP3A4 inhibitor — affects cyclosporine, hormonal contraceptives (reduced efficacy), glyburide.

requirements: Prescriber must be enrolled in Letairis REMS (LEAP program)
Female patients of reproductive potential: negative pregnancy test before starting and monthly thereafter
Effective contraception required
Dispense only through certified pharmacies enrolled in LEAP

monitoring_labs.interval: As clinically indicated (lower hepatotoxicity risk than bosentan)

notes: Ambrisentan is an endothelin receptor antagonist like bosentan but more selective (ETA). Lower hepatotoxicity risk than bosentan — routine monthly LFT monitoring no longer required, but still recommended at baseline. Teratogenic — pregnancy prevention is the primary REMS focus.

requirements: Prescriber must be enrolled in Opsumit REMS
Female patients of reproductive potential: negative pregnancy test before starting and monthly thereafter
Effective contraception required during and for 1 month after treatment
Dispense only through certified pharmacies
notes: Macitentan is a dual endothelin receptor antagonist. Teratogenic — contraindicated in pregnancy. Less hepatotoxicity than bosentan. Decreases hemoglobin — monitor if anemia develops. Do not use with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors.

medications: Mifepristone 200 mg (Mifeprex) — for medical termination of pregnancy
requirements: Prescriber must be certified in Mifeprex REMS
Must be able to date pregnancies accurately (ultrasound if needed)
Must be able to provide or arrange for surgical intervention if incomplete termination or emergency
Patient must sign Patient Agreement Form
Dispensed directly to patient by or under supervision of certified prescriber — not through retail pharmacies
Follow-up assessment required within 7-14 days to confirm complete termination

monitoring_labs.lab: Follow-up assessment (clinical exam or serum hCG)

notes: Mifeprex is approved for termination of intrauterine pregnancy through 70 days (10 weeks) gestation. Used in combination with misoprostol. Risk of serious infection (including fatal cases of Clostridium sordellii). Rh testing and Rh immunoglobulin may be needed. This REMS is separate from the Korlym REMS for mifepristone 300 mg used in Cushing syndrome.

medications: Mifepristone 300 mg (Korlym) — for Cushing syndrome hyperglycemia
requirements: Prescriber must be certified in Korlym REMS
Female patients: negative pregnancy test before starting, MUST use non-hormonal contraception
NOTE: Mifepristone blocks progesterone — hormonal contraception will NOT work
Pregnancy testing before each prescription refill
Dispense only from certified pharmacy
Patient must sign Patient-Prescriber Agreement

monitoring_labs.interval: Before treatment and before each refill
Before treatment, within 1-2 weeks, and periodically

notes: Korlym mifepristone 300mg is DIFFERENT from Mifeprex mifepristone 200mg. Korlym is for endogenous Cushing syndrome with hyperglycemia/glucose intolerance. It blocks cortisol receptors — adrenal insufficiency can occur. Key interaction: mifepristone blocks progesterone, so hormonal contraception is ineffective and pregnancy tests may be unreliable. Use non-hormonal contraception ONLY.

requirements: Prescriber must be certified in Juxtapid REMS
Only for homozygous familial hypercholesterolemia (HoFH) — NOT for general LDL lowering
Patient must be enrolled in Juxtapid REMS registry
Liver function monitoring: LFTs before treatment, then per schedule
Advise patients to take daily vitamin E and essential fatty acid supplements

monitoring_labs.lab: ALT, AST, alkaline phosphatase, total bilirubin
monitoring_labs.interval: Before treatment, then monthly × first year, then every 3 months
Before treatment, annually, then at discontinuation

notes: Lomitapide inhibits microsomal triglyceride transfer protein (MTP). Causes hepatic fat accumulation (steatosis) — long-term consequences unknown. Hepatotoxicity risk — dose adjustment or discontinuation required if ALT > 5× ULN. Contraindicated in pregnancy and with moderate/strong CYP3A4 inhibitors. Very restricted patient population (HoFH only).

requirements: Hospital must be enrolled in Entereg REMS
HOSPITAL USE ONLY — patients must NOT receive more than 15 doses
NOT available through outpatient pharmacies
Maximum treatment duration: 7 days (in-hospital)
Not for patients who have received therapeutic doses of opioids for > 7 consecutive days before surgery
notes: Alvimopan is a peripherally acting mu-opioid receptor antagonist. Used to accelerate GI recovery after bowel resection surgery. Long-term use (beyond the surgical admission) was associated with higher risk of myocardial infarction in clinical trials — hence the strict hospital-only restriction.
